Designing Resilient Data Pipelines

Every data pipeline eventually fails. The network partitions, the upstream API changes its schema without warning, a disk fills up at three in the morning. The difference between a pipeline you can trust and one you babysit is not whether it fails, but how it behaves when it does. This article collects the patterns that kept our ingestion layer boring for three years.

Idempotency first

The single most valuable property a pipeline stage can have is idempotency. If every stage can be re-run safely, then recovery from any failure reduces to one operation: run it again. We enforce this by keying every write on a deterministic identifier derived from the source record, never on auto-increment counters or wall-clock timestamps.

def ingest(record: Record) -> None:
    key = sha256(record.source_id)
    store.upsert(key, record)

Backpressure beats buffering

When a downstream system slows, the worst response is to buffer indefinitely. Unbounded queues turn a latency problem into a memory problem and then into an outage. We cap every queue and propagate the slowdown upstream, letting the source throttle itself. The pipeline degrades gracefully instead of collapsing suddenly.

None of these patterns are novel, and that is the point. Boring techniques, applied consistently, are what resilience actually looks like in production.
